Source_Species.reason: The paper studies Magnaporthe oryzae throughout, including TPS1 expression analysis in this species.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Magnaporthe oryzae is explicitly the organism studied.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Guy-11 is the wild-type strain used for expression comparisons.

Applied_Species.reason: TPS1 expression was analyzed in M. oryzae mutants derived from Guy-11.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species is explicitly Magnaporthe oryzae.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Expression was compared in Guy-11 and glycogen-metabolic mutant derivatives (Δagl1, Δgph1, Δagl1Δgph1).

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: TPS1 is described as encoding trehalose-6-phosphate synthase and as part of a G6P/NADPH-responsive genetic switch, but the exact phrasing 'trehalose-6-phosphate synthase complex component; glucose-6-phosphate and NADPH-responsive switch protein' is a synthesis rather than a direct statement.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: TPS1 encodes trehalose-6-phosphate synthase, and the paper links TPS1 to trehalose biosynthesis.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Trehalose is a carbohydrate; this is standard and directly consistent with the paper's treatment of trehalose as a storage sugar.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper discusses trehalose as a major nutrient reserve/storage compound and links the TPS1 system to virulence regulation.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Trehalose levels were measured in mycelial extracts in Figure 7A.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: qRT-PCR showed TPS1 expression in Guy11 mycelium and ~5-fold reduced expression in Δagl1, Δgph1 and Δagl1Δgph1 mutants.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: This matches the reported expression analysis in wild type versus glycogen-catabolism mutants.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The Results state TPS1 expression is reduced in glycogen metabolic mutants, and the authors connect this to the Tps1-mediated virulence switch.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: TPS1 transcript levels were measured by comparative quantitative real-time RT-PCR, and the NMR3 deletion experiment was used genetically to support the regulatory link.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: TPS1 expression was measured in mycelia grown in complete medium; extension to infection-related development is interpretive rather than directly tested for TPS1 expression.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points reflect Results: ~5-fold TPS1 reduction, association with reduced trehalose/pathogenicity in glycogen mutants, and TPS1 as a mechanistic bridge in the proposed model.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: TPS3 is part of the same trehalose biosynthetic complex context, and NMR3 is part of the Tps1-associated regulatory system, but direct physical interaction of TPS1 with TPS3 or NMR3 is not demonstrated in this paper.

Other_Important_Info.reason: TPS1 was not directly mutated here, but its expression was explicitly measured and central to the mechanistic model advanced in the Results/Discussion.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper supports TPS1's central role in virulence regulation, but 'strong candidate antifungal target' is an application inference not explicitly tested.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper explicitly states TPS3 encodes a subunit of the trehalose phosphatase synthase/phosphatase complex.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: TPS3 is directly tied to the trehalose biosynthetic machinery in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Trehalose is a carbohydrate, matching the paper's context.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Trehalose is discussed as a storage carbohydrate and in a virulence-associated metabolic context.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Trehalose was quantified in mycelial extracts.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: TPS3 was expressed in Guy11 mycelium and reduced in Δagl1, Δgph1, and especially Δagl1Δgph1 mutants.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: This matches the Figure 7C expression data in glycogen-catabolism mutants.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The Results explicitly report marked TPS3 downregulation in glycogen catabolic mutants accompanying reduced trehalose levels.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: TPS3 transcript levels were measured by comparative quantitative real-time RT-PCR.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The gene expression analysis section states mycelia were grown in complete medium for 48 h before RNA extraction.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: The fold-change values (6-fold, 9-fold, 50-fold) and linkage to reduced trehalose accumulation are explicitly stated; the conclusion that glycogen degradation influences trehalose biosynthetic machinery is directly supported.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: TPS1 and TPS3 are described as components of the same complex/system, but direct protein-protein interaction is not shown in this paper.

Other_Important_Info.reason: TPS3 was measured as an expression readout and not directly mutated in this study.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The paper explicitly identifies AGL1 as MGG_00063.6.

Source_Species.reason: AGL1 was identified and tested in Magnaporthe oryzae.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The species is explicitly Magnaporthe oryzae.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Targeted deletion and other experiments used the Guy-11 strain.

Applied_Species.reason: Functional analyses were performed in M. oryzae.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species is Magnaporthe oryzae.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: AGL1 deletion and complementation/localization experiments were done in Guy-11 background.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: AGL1 is presented as a glycogen degradation gene, and its loss is linked to trehalose metabolism changes.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper explicitly refers to cytosolic glycogen breakdown and places AGL1 in this route.

Enzyme_Name.reason: AGL1 is explicitly described as encoding amyloglucosidase.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is given for AGL1 in the paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper reports conserved motifs of the α-amylase superfamily and four conserved stretches in the α/β barrel domain.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The Results state amyloglucosidase removes α-1,6-glycosidic bonds from branched glycogen to release glucose.

Primary_Substrate.reason: AGL1 acts on glycogen according to the paper.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper states amyloglucosidase releases glucose.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: It is described as part of cytosolic glycogen mobilization/breakdown.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Deletion of AGL1 reduces trehalose and TPS1/TPS3 expression, supporting a connection via precursor/regulatory support to the trehalose pathway.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports AGL1 as a key glycogen-mobilizing enzyme important for pathogenicity, while not formally establishing rate limitation; the field wording preserves that caveat.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: AGL1 deletion is linked in Results to altered trehalose levels.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper treats trehalose as a storage carbohydrate and ties its biosynthetic pathway to virulence regulation.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Trehalose was measured in mycelium, while glycogen was visualized in conidia/appressoria. The combined site list 'Mycelium; conidia; appressoria' mixes trehalose and glycogen locations.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Agl1-GFP was observed in conidia, germ tubes, appressoria, and invasive hyphae.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: This matches the localization/expression observations for Agl1-GFP in the experimental Guy11 background.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Δagl1 mutants showed delayed glycogen mobilization, reduced pathogenicity, reduced trehalose, and reduced TPS1/TPS3 expression.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are described and used: targeted deletion, enzyme assay, GFP localization, glycogen staining/quantification, pathogenicity assay, qRT-PCR, and complementation.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Complementation used full-length genes under native promoters, and GFP fusion constructs used endogenous promoter context.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These are the exact developmental and infection contexts tested for AGL1.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each point is supported by Results: cytoplasmic amyloglucosidase role in glycogen mobilization, reduced trehalose and ~5-fold TPS1/~6-fold TPS3 downregulation, and reduced lesion formation with impaired invasive growth.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Functional/genetic relationships with GPH1, TPS1, TPS3, and NMR3 are supported, but direct physical interactions are not shown.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper reports normal germination/appressorium formation, no significant turgor or penetration defect, and complementation restoring growth on starch.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Potential antifungal target is a reasonable inference but not explicitly tested as an application in the paper.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Δagl1 mutants were compromised in utilizing glycogen/starch as sole carbon sources and showed reduced invasive growth.

corrections.reason: No EC number is given for AGL1 in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper presents trehalose as a storage carbohydrate and part of a virulence-associated metabolic/regulatory context.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The paper supports genetic/functional links with AGL1, TPS1, TPS3, and NMR3, but not direct physical interactions.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: GSN1 is involved in glycogen synthesis/storage.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper explicitly identifies GSN1 as encoding glycogen synthase.

Primary_Product.reason: As glycogen synthase, GSN1 is directly linked to glycogen synthesis, and deletion reduces glycogen accumulation.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: GSN1 is clearly the core anabolic step of glycogen synthesis/storage in the paper's framing.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper's Results support that targeting glycogen synthesis alone would likely have low value for disease suppression because Δgsn1 retained pathogenicity.

Source_Species.reason: NMR3 deletion was tested in Magnaporthe oryzae.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The species is explicitly Magnaporthe oryzae.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: The NMR3 experiment used a Guy-11-derived mutant background (Δagl1Δgph1).

Applied_Species.reason: NMR3 was tested in M. oryzae during infection assays.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species is Magnaporthe oryzae.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Deletion was made in a Guy-11-derived Δagl1Δgph1 background.

Regulator_Type.reason: The paper explicitly calls NMR3 a transcriptional inhibitor gene.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper refers to Nmr proteins as NADP-dependent Nmr inhibitor proteins; NMR3 is one of these transcriptional inhibitors.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: The paper links NMR3 to the Tps1-associated system integrating carbon/nitrogen metabolism and virulence-associated gene regulation.

Regulation_Mode.reason: NMR proteins are described as negative regulators/inhibitors in the Tps1 genetic switch controlling virulence-associated gene expression.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: The paper states Nmr inhibitor proteins modulate virulence-associated gene expression through the Tps1 system.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: Deletion of NMR3 relieves inhibition sufficiently to restore virulence in the mutant background, consistent with inhibitory function.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: Deletion of NMR3 partially restored virulence to the Δagl1Δgph1 mutant, directly supporting a decisive negative role in the glycogen/Tps1 regulatory axis.

Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation.reason: The paper supports NMR3 as part of the NADPH-dependent Tps1 switch, but does not directly establish a specific feedback/feedforward architecture for NMR3 itself.

Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs.reason: The Tps1 system is explicitly stated to sense glucose-6-phosphate and NADPH/NADP balance, and NMR3 acts in that associated network.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: 'Trehalose-associated virulence regulatory state' is not a metabolite and is not a paper-stated terminal metabolite.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Because the terminal metabolite field is not a true metabolite, classifying it as 'Carbohydrate' is not supported.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper links trehalose pathway components to pathogenicity regulation, but this field refers to a regulatory state rather than an actual measured metabolite.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The Results explicitly state that deletion of NMR3 partially restores pathogenicity to the Δagl1Δgph1 mutant.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: NMR3 was deleted in the double-mutant background and pathogenicity tested on rice and barley.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The virulence assays were conducted on rice CO-39 and barley Golden Promise.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points match the Results, though 'close to wild type' should be interpreted cautiously; the paper says no significant lesion number difference versus Guy11 in those assays and partial restoration/small lesions.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: NMR3 is functionally associated with TPS1 and linked genetically to AGL1/GPH1 effects, but direct protein interactions are not shown.

Protein_Complex_Involvement.reason: NMR3 is part of the Tps1-regulated inhibitory system, but a defined physical protein complex involving NMR3 is not demonstrated here.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly explains that NMR3 was chosen because prior work showed NMR deletions restore pathogenicity to Δtps1 mutants, and here it was used to test linkage to the Tps1 switch.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper supports NMR3 as a virulence regulator, and the caution that its deletion increases pathogenicity in this background is consistent with the data.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Deletion restored virulence in the glycogen-catabolism-defective background, indicating possible enhancement of disease severity rather than suppression.

corrections.reason: 'Trehalose-associated virulence regulatory state' is not a metabolite and is not a paper-stated terminal metabolite.
Because the terminal metabolite field is not a true metabolite, classifying it as 'Carbohydrate' is not supported.
